children.title: Monolithic Fabrication of Silicon Nanowires Bridging Thick Silicon Structures
children.metadata: Silicon nanowires; Monolithic fabrication; Anisotropic etching; Sidewall passivation; Nanomechanical characterization; Atomic force microscopy; SCREAM process; Microsystem integration
children.content: ZUHAL TASDEMIR\(^{1}\) (Member, IEEE), OLIVER PERIC\(^{2}\), DAVIDE SACCHETTO\(^{3}\) (Member, IEEE), GEORGE ERNEST FANTNER\(^{4}\), YUSUF LELEBRIFF (Fellow, IEEE), JUDITH KENNETH MURGEN\(^{5}\), MICHAEL M. HAYES\(^{6}\).<|txt_split|>\(^{1}\)Department of Mechanical Engineering, Koc University, Istanbul 34450, Turkey<|txt_split|>Laboratory for Bio- and Nano-Instrumentation, Ecole Polytechnique Federale de Luxarnc, Lusanne 1015, Switzerlnd<|txt_split|>Microelectronic Systems Laboratory, Ecole Polytechnique Feddale de Lausanne, Lausanne 1015, Switzerland<|txt_split|>4Surface Science and Technology Center, Koc University, Istanbul 34450, Turkey<|txt_split|>CORRESPONDING AUTHOR: B. E. ALACA (ealaca@ku.edu.tr)<|txt_split|>This work was supported in part by Tubukay under Grant 1212058 and in part by the Koc University -Istanbul-Italyotchia Club Fundamental Research Program, and by the European Union project No. 673749. The authors would like to thank G. Farmer for supported in part by the European Union FP7/2007-2015/ERC under Grant No. S07358-NaMc. This work was supported in part by the European Union project No. 673749.<|txt_split|>ABSTRACT A monolithic process is developed for the fabrication of Si nanowires within thick Si substrates. A combination of anisotropic etch and sidewall passivation is utilized to protect and release Si lines during the subsequent deep etch. An etch depth of \(10\mu \mathrm{m}\) is demonstrated with a future prospect for \(50~\mu \mathrm{m}\) opening up new possibilities for the deterministic integration of nanowires with microsystems. Nanowires with in-plane thicknesses as well as \(20\mathrm{nm}\) and aspect ratios up to 150 are obtained. Nanomechanical characterization through bending tests further confirms structural integrity of the connection between nanowires and anchoring Si microstructures.<|txt_split|>INDEX TERMS Atomic force microscopy (AFM), bending test, silicon nanowires, single crystal reactive etching and metallization (SCREAM).

children.title: I. INTRODUCTION
children.metadata: One-dimensional nanostructures; nanowires; MEMS integration; batch fabrication; silicon on insulator; multi-degree-of-freedom sensors; top-down fabrication; wafer surface processing
children.content: One-dimensional nanostructures have gained a considerable attention in the field of semiconductor technology [1]. With the help of rapid development in the process technology, the use of nanoscale devices has been realized with new capabilities [2]-[4]. One such area of interest is the integration of one-dimensional nanostructures, such as nanowires (NWS), with microelectromechanical systems (MEMS), where the addition of the nanoscale element brings up enhanced functionality [5] as exemplified by the proposed measurement resolution through to the contributions of Si NWs and microtubes in inert MEMS sensors [6].<|txt_split|>Combination of two entities, Si NW and MEMS, with a three-order of magnitude scale difference is difficult to carry out within the boundaries of the monolithic process. There are, of course, various alternatives including directional etching [7] or the possibility of growing Si NWs via vaporizing the Si NWs in a chemical reaction. However, these techniques after MEMS fabrication. However, none of these techniques have been able to meet the stringent requirements of batch fabrication so far. An exception is the top-down approach,<|txt_contd|>where Si NWs are fabricated within the thin device layer \((250~\mathrm{nm})\) of a silicon on insulator (SOI) wafer, on top of which a thick \((10~\mu \mathrm{m})\) poly-Si is deposited for MEMS fabrication [6]. The need for thin SOI and use of multiple Si layers increases demands on the already challenging fabrication process. This leads to the advantages of Si wafer deposition justly this extra effort. This approach already raised new opportunities in the miniaturization of multiple degree-of-freedom inertial sensors [10].<|txt_split|>In this work we explore the limits of batch fabricating a single NW within a thick Si layer with two objectives that go beyond the state of the art [6]. The Si NW is to be i) fabricated within the same crystal as MEMS- as opposed to a separate Si layer, and ii) kept at the wafer surface for the ease of further processing, such as doping and contact formation.

subnode.title: I. INTRODUCTION_1
subnode.metadata: One-dimensional nanostructures; nanowires; microelectromechanical systems; MEMS sensors; nanoscale integration; enhanced functionality
subnode.content: One-dimensional nanostructures have gained a considerable attention in the field of semiconductor technology [1]. With the help of rapid development in the process technology, the use of nanoscale devices has been realized with new capabilities [2]-[4]. One such area of interest is the integration of one-dimensional nanostructures, such as nanowires (NWS), with microelectromechanical systems (MEMS), where the addition of the nanoscale element brings up enhanced functionality [5] as exemplified by the proposed measurement resolution through to the contributions of Si NWs and microtubes in inert MEMS sensors [6].

subnode.title: I. INTRODUCTION_2
subnode.metadata: Si NW; MEMS; monolithic process; directional etching; vaporization; batch fabrication; top-down approach
subnode.content: Combination of two entities, Si NW and MEMS, with a three-order of magnitude scale difference is difficult to carry out within the boundaries of the monolithic process. There are, of course, various alternatives including directional etching [7] or the possibility of growing Si NWs via vaporizing the Si NWs in a chemical reaction. However, these techniques after MEMS fabrication. However, none of these techniques have been able to meet the stringent requirements of batch fabrication so far. An exception is the top-down approach,

subnode.title: I. INTRODUCTION_3
subnode.metadata: Si NWs fabrication; thin SOI device layer; poly-Si deposition; MEMS miniaturization; multi-degree-of-freedom sensors
subnode.content: where Si NWs are fabricated within the thin device layer \((250~\mathrm{nm})\) of a silicon on insulator (SOI) wafer, on top of which a thick \((10~\mu \mathrm{m})\) poly-Si is deposited for MEMS fabrication [6]. The need for thin SOI and use of multiple Si layers increases demands on the already challenging fabrication process. This leads to the advantages of Si wafer deposition justly this extra effort. This approach already raised new opportunities in the miniaturization of multiple degree-of-freedom inertial sensors [10].

subnode.title: I. INTRODUCTION_4
subnode.metadata: batch fabrication; single NW; thick Si layer; MEMS-integrated; wafer surface processing
subnode.content: In this work we explore the limits of batch fabricating a single NW within a thick Si layer with two objectives that go beyond the state of the art [6]. The Si NW is to be i) fabricated within the same crystal as MEMS- as opposed to a separate Si layer, and ii) kept at the wafer surface for the ease of further processing, such as doping and contact formation.

children.title: II. PROCESS DEVELOPMENT
children.metadata: SREAM process; sidewall passivation; isotropic release etch; Si nanowire formation; e-beam resist HQS; Cl₂ RIE etching; low-temperature oxide coating; fluorofluor plasma etching; DRIE scallop size; HF vapor etching
children.content: To achieve this set of objectives, we revisit SREAM (Single Crystal Reactive Etching and Metalization) process [11] that played an important role in the surface micromachining process. The main aim of this work is to investigate the critical sidewall passivation, which would provide protection<|txt_contd|>during isotropic release etch. Similarly, if one can define a nanoscale Si line protected with a conformal sidewall coating, one can, in principle, etch deep into the substrate transforming the protected line into a Si NW. How deep one can etch the substrate while preserving the integrity of the Si NW constitutes the major motivation of this work.<|txt_split|>The process begins with the definition of 200-nm-thick e-beam resist HQS (Hydrogen silsesquioxane) lines on a \(<100>\) Si wafer. Linewidths between \(20-80\mathrm{nm}\) are patterned parallel to \(\times 110>\) (Fig. 1a). This dimension directly translates into the Si NW width (in-plane dimension). HQS is used as an etch mask for the subsequent \(\mathrm{Cl}_2\) reactive ion etching process. The etching process is performed at \(150~\mathrm{nm}\) leading to the formation of Si lines (Fig. 1b). An \(80-\mathrm{nm}\) thick conformal coating of low-temperature oxide (LTO) is used for sidewall protection (Fig. 1c), which is then etched from lateral surfaces in a fluorofluor plasma (Fig. 1d).<|txt_split|>A deep reactive ion etching (DRIE) recipe is developed to produce an adequate scallop size allowing the release of Si NW at the end of the first cycle of etching (Fig. 1.e). Further cycles of etching are carried out to obtain a single \(\mathrm{SiO}_2\) layer with measured trench depth. Finally, both LTO and HSQ are removed by hydrofluoric acid (HF) vapor etching (Fig. 1.f).

children.title: FIGURE 1. Process flow for a single suspended \(5\mathrm{NM}\) This work aims to investigate the effect of the \(\alpha\) -DIBI on the surface morphology of \(20\mathrm{nm}\) and \(\alpha\) thickness of about \(100\mathrm{nm}\). (a) High-resolution lithography. (b) XRD patterns of the \(\alpha\) -DIBI films deposited on the surface of the \(\alpha\) -DIBI oxide (LO). (c) LO removal from horizontal surfaces. (d) DIBI with the same surface area as the \(\alpha\) -DIBI films. The surface areas were measured by electron microscopy, taken from it from its unprotected bottom surfaces. (e) Removal of protective layers.
children.metadata: Silicon nanowire, etching, lithography, conformal coating, DRIE, protective layers
children.content: The image illustrates the fabrication process of a single suspended silicon nanowire (Si NW), as detailed in Figure 1. It comprises six labeled steps: (a) starting with a Si substrate, (b) an etch depth of 10 µm while preserving a minimum Si NW width of 20 nm and thickness of about 100 nm, (c) high-resolution lithography, (d) anisotropic etching, (e) conformal coating of low-temperature oxide (LTO) to remove horizontal surfaces, and (f) DRIE with adequate undercut to release the Si NW while avoiding significant damage to its unprotected bottom surface. The diagram includes dimension annotations, such as 20–20 nm for the Si NW width and 100 nm for thickness, and a schematic of the etch profile.

subnode.title: II. PROCESS DEVELOPMENT_1
subnode.metadata: SREAM process; sidewall passivation; isotropic release etch; nanoscale Si line; Si nanowire fabrication; substrate etching depth
subnode.content: To achieve this set of objectives, we revisit SREAM (Single Crystal Reactive Etching and Metalization) process [11] that played an important role in the surface micromachining process. The main aim of this work is to investigate the critical sidewall passivation, which would provide protectionduring isotropic release etch. Similarly, if one can define a nanoscale Si line protected with a conformal sidewall coating, one can, in principle, etch deep into the substrate transforming the protected line into a Si NW. How deep one can etch the substrate while preserving the integrity of the Si NW constitutes the major motivation of this work.

subnode.title: II. PROCESS DEVELOPMENT_2
subnode.metadata: e-beam resist HQS patterning; Si NW fabrication; Cl₂ RIE etching; LTO conformal coating; fluorofluor plasma etching
subnode.content: The process begins with the definition of 200-nm-thick e-beam resist HQS (Hydrogen silsesquioxane) lines on a \(<100>\) Si wafer. Linewidths between \(20-80\mathrm{nm}\) are patterned parallel to \(\times 110>\) (Fig. 1a). This dimension directly translates into the Si NW width (in-plane dimension). HQS is used as an etch mask for the subsequent \(\mathrm{Cl}_2\) reactive ion etching process. The etching process is performed at \(150~\mathrm{nm}\) leading to the formation of Si lines (Fig. 1b). An \(80-\mathrm{nm}\) thick conformal coating of low-temperature oxide (LTO) is used for sidewall protection (Fig. 1c), which is then etched from lateral surfaces in a fluorofluor plasma (Fig. 1d).

subnode.title: II. PROCESS DEVELOPMENT_3
subnode.metadata: DRIE recipe development; Si NW release; single SiO₂ layer formation; LTO and HSQ removal via HF vapor etching
subnode.content: A deep reactive ion etching (DRIE) recipe is developed to produce an adequate scallop size allowing the release of Si NW at the end of the first cycle of etching (Fig. 1.e). Further cycles of etching are carried out to obtain a single \(\mathrm{SiO}_2\) layer with measured trench depth. Finally, both LTO and HSQ are removed by hydrofluoric acid (HF) vapor etching (Fig. 1.f).

children.title: A. SILICON NANOWIRE
children.metadata: Si NWs; etch depth; aspect ratio; width uniformity; thickness variation; DRIE process; HSQ limitation; isotropic etching; FIB cross-section; anchor effect; whisker remnants; linewidth control; Si NW release; recipe optimization
children.content: An array of resulting Si NWs is shown in Fig. 2a. The process flow yields a \(10 - \mu \mathrm{m}\) etch depth, a promising achievement as a device thickness of \(10\mu \mathrm{m}\) is frequently used in all-Si NPs. The Si NWs are formed by the process of etching and spanned by Si NWs with widths between \(20 - 80\mathrm{nm}\). An aspect ratio (NW length/width) range of 10-150 is achieved leading to Si NW lengths between \(200\mathrm{nm}\) and \(12\mu \mathrm{m}\). A process yield above \(90\%\) is obtained, indicating high repeatability and high reproducibility with laboratory and hence suitability for bare fabrication.<|txt_split|>Fig. 2.b and 2.c depict a Si NW with the smallest width of \(20~\mathrm{nm}\) and a moderate width of \(30~\mathrm{nm}\) respectively.<|txt_split|>Average Si NW thickness (out-of-plane dimension defined by the first RIE step of Fig. 1b) is observed to be \(100\mathrm{nm}\) close-up provided in the inset). The reduction of the original \(150~\mathrm{nm}\) thickness related to RIE etch depth (Fig. 1b) to \(100\mathrm{nm}\) after DRIE (Fig. 1c) is an indication of isotropic etching. In addition, the increase in the RIE etch depth of the Si NW is further thinned down to \(95~\mathrm{nm}\), whereas near the anchors the thickness increases to \(107\mathrm{nm}\). As etching from the bottom takes place in a mass-transport-limited fashion, Si NW misdirection is exposed to a higher isotropic etching rate due to faster diffusion away from the supports. Under the conditions reported above, a thickness of \(200\mathrm{nm}\) is obtained controlled by the etching of the specific etch recipe, achieving such dimensional uniformity in thickness has been more challenging compared to the uniformity in width.<|txt_split|>HSQ thickness is observed to be the main limiting factor for the etch depth, as prolonged etching leads to the loss of HSQ. Etch mask material or materials can be tailored, if a need for thicker devices, e.g. \(50 - \mu \mathrm{m}\) -thick SOLi arises. In case of a more resistant etch mask the loss of Si from the unprotected Si NW bottom surface would be critical as explained above. Replacing DRIE with an isotropic release step such as a high-pressure RIB was previously observed in the Si NW bottom surface. The etch thickness and etch depth [12]. An optimization of the interplay among the initial Si line thickness (Fig. 1b) and the isotropic etch component of DRIE can be further carried out for a desired combination of Si NW thickness and overall etch depth.<|txt_split|>The importance of recipe optimization is further exemplified in Fig. 2d showing a Si NW with the maximum width defined by the lithography of Fig. 1a and LTO sidewall coating thickness of Fig. 1d sets the maximum linewidth to be etched for a complete Si NW release. In this work, we have demonstrated that the Si NWs can be obtained. In this study, discontinuous, residual Si whiskers, remnants<|txt_contd|>from DRIE of Fig. 1, i.e., survive for a NW width of \(80~\mathrm{nm}\). For all widths smaller, the undercut associated with DRIE recipe successfully removes Si underneath the Si NW. On the contrary, further reduction of the isotropic etch component will lead to the formation of Si NW arrays as previously observed in the form of Si NW stacks [13].<|txt_split|>A further cross-sectional study is carried out by using Focused Ion Beam (FIB) milling. Prior to FIB, Si NWs are coated with a \(20\mathrm{-mm}\)-thick Pt layer by atomic layer deposition (ALD) to enhance contrast during scanning electron imaging. 10 SI NWs with widths of \(20 - 40\mathrm{nm}\) and thicknesses of \(10 - 30\mathrm{nm}\) were used. The cross-section was imaged. Fig. 3a depicts a \(40\mathrm{-nm}\) wide Si NW with an aspect ratio of 50 cut both at its center and near the anchor. Si NWs exhibit a rectangular cross-section with the maximum thickness at the anchors. Thickness is observed to decrease by \(10\%\) towards the middle, as discussed in Fig. 2b. The width also increases by \(10\%\) towards anchors due to the backscattering effect in electron beam lithography [14]. The increase of both the width and the thickness from the center (Fig. 3b) to the anchor (Fig. 3c) is evident in cross-sectional micrographs. A close-up near the center of the cross-section shows that it is a rectangular cross-section. This cross-section is different from the scallop profile as depicted in Fig. 1f. This difference hints at the relatively large size of the scallop radius of curvature compared to the NW width.

children.title: FIGURE 2. (a) An array of Si NWs anchored between two \(10\mu \mathrm{m}\)-thick, \(0.5\mu \mathrm{m}\)-thick arrays with a thickness of \(10\mu \mathrm{m}\) (thickness of \(2.5\mathrm{nm}\)) and an array of \(20\mu \mathrm{m}\) (thickness of \(23.7\mathrm{nm}\) in a Zeiss Marxel SIM). (b) A Si NW with a width of \(40\mathrm{nm}\) in a Si NW with the maximum width of \(80\mathrm{nm}\). (c) A Si NW with a width of \(100\mathrm{nm}\) in a Si NW with the maximum width of \(200\mathrm{nm}\). Inset indicates their location with respect to the Si NW.
children.metadata: Silicon nanowires, triangular supports, SEM images, width measurement, Zeiss Merlin, DRII remnants
children.content: The image displays four SEM micrographs of silicon nanowires (Si NWs) with varying widths and structural features. Panel (a) shows an array of Si NWs anchored between two 10-µm-thick triangular supports. Panel (b) presents a Si NW with the minimum achievable width of 20 nm, measured as 23.7 nm in a Zeiss Merlin SEM. Panel (c) shows a Si NW with a moderate width of 30 nm. Panel (d) depicts a Si NW with the maximum width of 80 nm. Insets in each panel indicate their respective locations, and remnants of DRII (dissociative reaction-induced interface) in the form of discontinuous Si whiskers are visible.

children.title: Scanning Electron Microscope Image of Nanoscale Structures
children.metadata: Scanning electron microscopy; nanoscale; surface morphology; arrows; 4 µm scale bar
children.content: The image is a scanning electron microscopy (SEM) view of a nanoscale surface, likely a semiconductor or material sample, showing two distinct regions labeled A and B, each with corresponding arrows indicating directional features or surface texture. The arrows suggest movement or orientation of surface features, possibly indicating crystal growth or etching directions. A scale bar labeled "4 µm" is present at the bottom right, indicating the scale of the image. The surface appears textured with fine, layered structures, and the image is marked with "(a)" in the top left corner, suggesting it is part of a series or figure.

children.title: Electron Micrograph of Cellular Structure
children.metadata: Electron microscopy; cellular structure; 70 nm; cross-section; arrow
children.content: The image is an electron micrograph showing a cross-sectional view of a cellular structure, with a labeled 70 nm width indicated by arrows pointing to a central region. The structure appears as a vertically oriented, elongated, and somewhat cylindrical feature, surrounded by a network of intersecting lines or filaments, suggesting a complex internal architecture. The "A-A'" notation indicates a cross-sectional plane through the structure. The overall image is in grayscale, typical of electron microscopy, with high contrast highlighting the internal details of the cellular component.

children.title: Microscopic View of Nanoscale Structure
children.metadata: SEM image; nanowire; 80 nm scale; B-B' cross-section
children.content: This scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image shows a cross-sectional view of a nanoscale structure, likely a nanowire or nanotube, with a central pillar surrounded by symmetric, V-shaped grooves. An arrow indicates a width of 80 nm across the structure, and the label "B-B'" denotes the cross-sectional plane. The image reveals the fine topography and dimensionality of the nanomaterial at a high magnification.

children.title: FIGURE 3. (a) A \(40\mathrm{-nm}\) Si NW for FIB study, (b) FIB cut near the midsection \((A - R)\) revealing a width of \(70~\mathrm{nm}\) along an ALD-coated Pt thickness of about \(20~\mathrm{nm}\). (c) A cross-section near the anchor (B-F) showing the cross-section of the sample in the center of the sample showing a rectangular Si core enveloped by the PI film.
children.metadata: Scanning electron microscopy; FIB; silicon nanowire; anchor; ALD; Pt coating; cross-section
children.content: The image shows three FIB-SEM micrographs of a 40-nm-wide silicon nanowire (Si NW) from a study: (a) a midsection (A–A’) revealing a width of 70 nm including an ALD-coated Pt thickness of about 20 nm; (b) a cross-section near the anchor (B–B’) indicating an increase in the NW width; and (c) a close-up near the center showing a rectangular Si core enveloped by the Pt film.

subnode.title: A. SILICON NANOWIRE_1
subnode.metadata: Si NWs; etch depth; aspect ratio; process yield; reproducibility
subnode.content: An array of resulting Si NWs is shown in Fig. 2a. The process flow yields a \(10 - \mu \mathrm{m}\) etch depth, a promising achievement as a device thickness of \(10\mu \mathrm{m}\) is frequently used in all-Si NPs. The Si NWs are formed by the process of etching and spanned by Si NWs with widths between \(20 - 80\mathrm{nm}\). An aspect ratio (NW length/width) range of 10-150 is achieved leading to Si NW lengths between \(200\mathrm{nm}\) and \(12\mu \mathrm{m}\). A process yield above \(90\%\) is obtained, indicating high repeatability and high reproducibility with laboratory and hence suitability for bare fabrication.

subnode.title: A. SILICON NANOWIRE_2
subnode.metadata: Si NW width; Si NW thickness; RIE etching; DRIE etching; isotropic etching; mass-transport-limited etching; dimensional uniformity
subnode.content: Fig. 2.b and 2.c depict a Si NW with the smallest width of \(20~\mathrm{nm}\) and a moderate width of \(30~\mathrm{nm}\) respectively.Average Si NW thickness (out-of-plane dimension defined by the first RIE step of Fig. 1b) is observed to be \(100\mathrm{nm}\) close-up provided in the inset). The reduction of the original \(150~\mathrm{nm}\) thickness related to RIE etch depth (Fig. 1b) to \(100\mathrm{nm}\) after DRIE (Fig. 1c) is an indication of isotropic etching. In addition, the increase in the RIE etch depth of the Si NW is further thinned down to \(95~\mathrm{nm}\), whereas near the anchors the thickness increases to \(107\mathrm{nm}\). As etching from the bottom takes place in a mass-transport-limited fashion, Si NW misdirection is exposed to a higher isotropic etching rate due to faster diffusion away from the supports. Under the conditions reported above, a thickness of \(200\mathrm{nm}\) is obtained controlled by the etching of the specific etch recipe, achieving such dimensional uniformity in thickness has been more challenging compared to the uniformity in width.

subnode.title: A. SILICON NANOWIRE_3
subnode.metadata: HSQ thickness limitation; etch depth control; etch mask material optimization; Si NW bottom surface protection; isotropic release alternative; DRIE optimization
subnode.content: HSQ thickness is observed to be the main limiting factor for the etch depth, as prolonged etching leads to the loss of HSQ. Etch mask material or materials can be tailored, if a need for thicker devices, e.g. \(50 - \mu \mathrm{m}\) -thick SOLi arises. In case of a more resistant etch mask the loss of Si from the unprotected Si NW bottom surface would be critical as explained above. Replacing DRIE with an isotropic release step such as a high-pressure RIB was previously observed in the Si NW bottom surface. The etch thickness and etch depth [12]. An optimization of the interplay among the initial Si line thickness (Fig. 1b) and the isotropic etch component of DRIE can be further carried out for a desired combination of Si NW thickness and overall etch depth.

subnode.title: A. SILICON NANOWIRE_4
subnode.metadata: Si NW optimization; lithography limitation; DRIE undercut; isotropic etch control; residual whiskers; NW width reduction; Si NW arrays
subnode.content: The importance of recipe optimization is further exemplified in Fig. 2d showing a Si NW with the maximum width defined by the lithography of Fig. 1a and LTO sidewall coating thickness of Fig. 1d sets the maximum linewidth to be etched for a complete Si NW release. In this work, we have demonstrated that the Si NWs can be obtained. In this study, discontinuous, residual Si whiskers, remnantsfrom DRIE of Fig. 1, i.e., survive for a NW width of \(80~\mathrm{nm}\). For all widths smaller, the undercut associated with DRIE recipe successfully removes Si underneath the Si NW. On the contrary, further reduction of the isotropic etch component will lead to the formation of Si NW arrays as previously observed in the form of Si NW stacks [13].

subnode.title: A. SILICON NANOWIRE_5
subnode.metadata: FIB milling; Pt coating via ALD; Si NW cross-section; rectangular profile; anchor thickening; width increase; backscattering effect; scallop profile comparison; aspect ratio 50
subnode.content: A further cross-sectional study is carried out by using Focused Ion Beam (FIB) milling. Prior to FIB, Si NWs are coated with a \(20\mathrm{-mm}\)-thick Pt layer by atomic layer deposition (ALD) to enhance contrast during scanning electron imaging. 10 SI NWs with widths of \(20 - 40\mathrm{nm}\) and thicknesses of \(10 - 30\mathrm{nm}\) were used. The cross-section was imaged. Fig. 3a depicts a \(40\mathrm{-nm}\) wide Si NW with an aspect ratio of 50 cut both at its center and near the anchor. Si NWs exhibit a rectangular cross-section with the maximum thickness at the anchors. Thickness is observed to decrease by \(10\%\) towards the middle, as discussed in Fig. 2b. The width also increases by \(10\%\) towards anchors due to the backscattering effect in electron beam lithography [14]. The increase of both the width and the thickness from the center (Fig. 3b) to the anchor (Fig. 3c) is evident in cross-sectional micrographs. A close-up near the center of the cross-section shows that it is a rectangular cross-section. This cross-section is different from the scallop profile as depicted in Fig. 1f. This difference hints at the relatively large size of the scallop radius of curvature compared to the NW width.

children.title: B. NANOMECHANICAL CHARACTERIZATION
children.metadata: AFM bending tests; Si NW mechanical properties; surface stress effects; finite element modeling; fracture behavior; intrinsic stress; nanoscale stiffness; tip-sample interactions; grain size deformation; uniaxial stress exceeding bulk strength
children.content: Resulting structures are tested through bending experiments in an atomic force microscope (AFM) through Bruker Peak Force QNM (Quantitative Nano Mechanical Mapping), and the results are compared with those obtained by the same correction factors are taken into account through the native<|txt_contd|>AFM software for cantilever (Bruker FastScana tip) stiffness and deflection sensitivity. During subsequent imaging, the AFM tip is positioned in the middle of the Si NW, first in a single direction, and then in the other, with the point of maximum deflection upon consecutive zooming.<|txt_split|>Imaging is followed by the ramping mode in which force is applied gradually until fracture at \(9\mu \mathrm{N}\) (Fig. 4a). Elastic behavior of Si NW is evident, where each loading with increased set-point force overlaps with previous ones. Further tests are performed on 4 Si NWs with a width of \(30~\mathrm{nm}\) and thickness of \(1.5\mu \mathrm{m}\) (Fig. 4b). The average stiffness exhibits a four-fold increase when compared to the expected value with the bulk modulus of elasticity of \(169\mathrm{GPa}\) taken for \(< 110>\) Si.<|txt_split|>Such stiffening is reported for double-clamped NwS, where surface stress is claimed to lead to an increase in the apparent modulus with higher aspect ratios [15]. As surface stresses are a key parameter in determining the strength of the filamentation, a tensile stress develops increasing the flexural stiffness. In addition, fabrication-related defects and intrinsic stresses develop [16]. Recent studies hint at the misinterpretation of the intrinsic stress effect as an increase in the tensile stress. This is not the case in the case of the finite element and intrinsic stress have to be extracted from measurements. As no appreciable change in the modulus is predicted for Si at the scale of interest of this work [19]-[21], the stress effect is modeled using finite element analysis with the bulk modulus. The finite element model is based on tetrahedral models [22] and is used to describe the influence of boundary condition. Experimental stiffness is matched, when this tensile intrinsic stress reaches \(850\mathrm{MPa}\). As such high intrinsic stresses are common in nanofabrication [21], [22], this study confirms the necessity to incorporate them in nanomechanical models. However, one should exercise caution when interpreting these results. For example, the various variations, shown to be as much as \(10\%\), and the lack of<|txt_contd|>modeling of the actual tip shape and tip-sample interactions constitute sources of uncertainty.<|txt_split|>As Si NWs and anchors are etched from the same crystal, a high-level of structural integrity is expected. Accordingly, in 14 out of 17 tests, fracture is observed to take place at the end of the etching process. The average grain size for Si NWs was \(0.18\mu \mathrm{m}\) where the deformation is still linear (Fig. 4b). the maximum uniaxial stress is found to be already on the order of \(2\mathrm{GPa}\) (Fig. 4c) surpassing the strength of bulk Si. Using the same finite element model with geometric non-adiabatic approximation, the fracture length is estimated to exceed previously reported \(12\mathrm{GPa}\) [23].

children.title: Force-Displacement Curves in Nanoscale Testing
children.metadata: Nanoscale testing; force-displacement curves; SEM image; red and blue lines; inset micrograph
children.content: The image displays two force-displacement plots from nanoscale mechanical testing. The left plot shows a force (in µN) versus displacement (in µm) curve with a red line and a blue line, alongside an inset SEM micrograph of a textured surface. The right plot shows a similar force-displacement curve with multiple colored lines (red, blue, green) illustrating different testing conditions or material responses. The curves exhibit distinct initial linear regions followed by nonlinear behavior, indicating material deformation under load. The inset micrograph provides a close-up view of the surface texture at the tested scale.

children.title: Force-Displacement Relationship
children.metadata: Force, displacement, experimental, model, graph
children.content: The image displays a linear graph plotting Force (in μN) against Displacement (in nm), with two data series: "Experimental" and "Model". The experimental data points are represented by a solid line, while the model data is shown as a dashed line, both trending upward from the origin. The graph spans from 0 to 50 nm on the displacement axis and 0 to 2.00 μN on the force axis. The model line appears slightly above the experimental line, indicating a slight discrepancy between the two.

children.title: FIGURE 4. (a) Mid-span loading curves to three different set-point forces, and (b) the corresponding stress distribution in the \(1\mathrm{mm}\) of the load (inset). Hysteresis associated with loading to \(0.5\mu \mathrm{N}\) and unloading is provided in a separate inset, (b) Loading curve obtained for our four NINs under the same regime. (c) Uniaxial stress distribution in SI NH mid-span for \(0.18\mu \mathrm{N}\).
children.metadata: Silicon nanowires, uniaxial loading, fracture, hysteresis, mid-span, set-point forces, stress distribution
children.content: The image presents three subplots (a, b, c) related to the mechanical behavior of silicon nanowires (Si NWs) under uniaxial loading. Subplot (a) shows mid-span loading curves for three different set-point forces, with fracture occurring at 9 µN at the point of loading (microscope inset). The hysteresis associated with loading (0.5 µN) and unloading is provided in a separate figure. Subplot (b) illustrates the loading curve obtained on four Si NWs in the linear regime. Subplot (c) depicts the uniaxial stress distribution along the mid-span of a Si NW. The diagram includes a schematic of the loading setup with arrows indicating force direction and magnitudes.

subnode.title: B. NANOMECHANICAL CHARACTERIZATION_1
subnode.metadata: AFM bending experiments; Bruker Peak Force QNM; nativeAFM software; cantilever stiffness; deflection sensitivity; Si NW imaging; maximum deflection; directional scanning
subnode.content: Resulting structures are tested through bending experiments in an atomic force microscope (AFM) through Bruker Peak Force QNM (Quantitative Nano Mechanical Mapping), and the results are compared with those obtained by the same correction factors are taken into account through the nativeAFM software for cantilever (Bruker FastScana tip) stiffness and deflection sensitivity. During subsequent imaging, the AFM tip is positioned in the middle of the Si NW, first in a single direction, and then in the other, with the point of maximum deflection upon consecutive zooming.

subnode.title: B. NANOMECHANICAL CHARACTERIZATION_2
subnode.metadata: Ramping mode imaging; Force application until fracture; Elastic behavior of Si NWs; Stiffness increase in Si NWs; Bulk modulus comparison
subnode.content: Imaging is followed by the ramping mode in which force is applied gradually until fracture at \(9\mu \mathrm{N}\) (Fig. 4a). Elastic behavior of Si NW is evident, where each loading with increased set-point force overlaps with previous ones. Further tests are performed on 4 Si NWs with a width of \(30~\mathrm{nm}\) and thickness of \(1.5\mu \mathrm{m}\) (Fig. 4b). The average stiffness exhibits a four-fold increase when compared to the expected value with the bulk modulus of elasticity of \(169\mathrm{GPa}\) taken for \(< 110>\) Si.

subnode.title: B. NANOMECHANICAL CHARACTERIZATION_3
subnode.metadata: surface stress; tensile stress; intrinsic stress; finite element analysis; nanofabrication; flexural stiffness; modulus increase; boundary condition; nanomechanical modeling
subnode.content: Such stiffening is reported for double-clamped NwS, where surface stress is claimed to lead to an increase in the apparent modulus with higher aspect ratios [15]. As surface stresses are a key parameter in determining the strength of the filamentation, a tensile stress develops increasing the flexural stiffness. In addition, fabrication-related defects and intrinsic stresses develop [16]. Recent studies hint at the misinterpretation of the intrinsic stress effect as an increase in the tensile stress. This is not the case in the case of the finite element and intrinsic stress have to be extracted from measurements. As no appreciable change in the modulus is predicted for Si at the scale of interest of this work [19]-[21], the stress effect is modeled using finite element analysis with the bulk modulus. The finite element model is based on tetrahedral models [22] and is used to describe the influence of boundary condition. Experimental stiffness is matched, when this tensile intrinsic stress reaches \(850\mathrm{MPa}\). As such high intrinsic stresses are common in nanofabrication [21], [22], this study confirms the necessity to incorporate them in nanomechanical models. However, one should exercise caution when interpreting these results. For example, the various variations, shown to be as much as \(10\%\), and the lack of

subnode.title: B. NANOMECHANICAL CHARACTERIZATION_4
subnode.metadata: tip shape modeling; tip-sample interactions; structural integrity; fracture at etching end; grain size 0.18μm; uniaxial stress 2GPa; bulk Si strength; fracture length estimation
subnode.content: modeling of the actual tip shape and tip-sample interactions constitute sources of uncertainty.As Si NWs and anchors are etched from the same crystal, a high-level of structural integrity is expected. Accordingly, in 14 out of 17 tests, fracture is observed to take place at the end of the etching process. The average grain size for Si NWs was \(0.18\mu \mathrm{m}\) where the deformation is still linear (Fig. 4b). the maximum uniaxial stress is found to be already on the order of \(2\mathrm{GPa}\) (Fig. 4c) surpassing the strength of bulk Si. Using the same finite element model with geometric non-adiabatic approximation, the fracture length is estimated to exceed previously reported \(12\mathrm{GPa}\) [23].

children.title: IV. CONCLUSION
children.metadata: Si NW monolithic fabrication; 20 nm narrow Si NWs; 150 aspect ratio; thickness control via etch; AFM mechanical testing; buried Si NWs; MEMS shuttle integration
children.content: A technique for the monolithic fabrication of Si NWs within \(10 - \mu \mathrm{m}\) thick Si layers is developed. SiNWs as narrow as \(20\mathrm{nm}\) are demonstrated with a maximum aspect ratio of 150. Thickness control is achieved through a combination of Si etch processes. Their mechanical integrity is tested using AFM bending tests. As the Si NW does not remain buried at the bottom of a thick Si layer, one can now pursue a set of experiments to determine the contact formation apart from MEMS fabrication. This ability constitutes an advantage for applications, where the integration of Si NWs with thicker MEMS shuttles is of interest.

subnode.title: Monolithic Fabrication of Silicon Nanowires Bridging Thick Silicon Structures_3
subnode.metadata: Si nanowires; monolithic fabrication; anisotropic etch; sidewall passivation; deep etch; nanomechanical characterization; deterministic integration
subnode.content: ABSTRACT A monolithic process is developed for the fabrication of Si nanowires within thick Si substrates. A combination of anisotropic etch and sidewall passivation is utilized to protect and release Si lines during the subsequent deep etch. An etch depth of \(10\mu \mathrm{m}\) is demonstrated with a future prospect for \(50~\mu \mathrm{m}\) opening up new possibilities for the deterministic integration of nanowires with microsystems. Nanowires with in-plane thicknesses as well as \(20\mathrm{nm}\) and aspect ratios up to 150 are obtained. Nanomechanical characterization through bending tests further confirms structural integrity of the connection between nanowires and anchoring Si microstructures.
